title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Public Works Committee approved several contracts and resolutions, including awarding a $126,900 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel, approving a state transportation funding resolution, and directing staff to create enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations with fines up to $100/day. The committee also approved construction agreements for Military Road utilities and construction services for upcoming road projects.

agenda.item: Acknowledge April 2026 water and sewer permit
Review 2025 Water Utility PSC annual report

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a Local Road Improvement Grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Scoffield Avenue and Community Center Drive, originally a 2029 project that will likely move to 2028. The grant covers over 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project. Michael noted the village has received over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion also covered Weston Avenue repairs by Integrity Grading, Scoffield Avenue repairs by HOS Construction, and Fuller Street repairs by Melvin. A special assessment meeting for Bladel Avenue was tentatively scheduled for Thursday, May 28th at 6:00 PM.

Staff reported road limits were removed April 29th along with Marathon County. Spring activities included mailbox repairs, sign repairs, curb line sweeping, and patching with hot mix asphalt. Storm damage from East Nick was addressed, primarily tree damage. Crews installed an additional 18-inch culvert on Jones Street after two culverts failed. Discussion addressed flooding issues near Weston Avenue requiring dewatering pumps for about a week; staff is exploring permanent pump solutions similar to a storm lift station. Salt supply is adequate with 1,500 tons ordered and 200 tons arriving this month.

Josh reported on water service repairs at Caribou Coffee, well maintenance, and private well sampling. Sanitary side experienced significant clear water infiltration - April showed 37 million gallons pumped into the system but 53-54 million gallons treated, indicating approximately 15 million gallons of clear water infiltration. Staff identified sump pump discharges into sanitary sewers as a major contributor. The committee discussed upcoming INI study by Inpipe Technologies to identify problem areas. Well two rehab started today, and water main flushing notices were mailed with flushing to begin next week.

Michael presented the annual report showing net operating income of $482,911 for 2025, consistent with 2024's $490,000. Water loss was 6%. Utility value increased to nearly $34 million with $6.7 million in infrastructure placed in service. The utility has approximately $6.8 million in cash reserves. The committee moved to acknowledge the report, which passed unanimously.

Michael explained the League of Wisconsin Municipalities drafted this resolution supporting long-term transportation funding solutions. The village has decreased public works budgets recently and needs state support for recurring costs like street maintenance and snow plowing. Wisconsin is noted as having relatively affordable automobile ownership compared to neighboring states. The committee voted unanimously to recommend the village board adopt Resolution 2026-014.

Michael presented the annual recycled asphalt crushing project, noting King Gravel was low bidder at $4.23/ton for an estimated 30,000 tons totaling $126,900. Analysis showed this saves approximately $11/cubic yard compared to City of Wausau's costs, equating to about $130,000 savings across three 2026 projects. The committee unanimously approved awarding the contract to King Gravel.

Staff sought guidance on enforcement of clear water discharge violations. Current code allows fines of $100-$1,000 per day. Committee members Zagami and Jordan distinguished between unknowing and willful violations. After discussion about waiting for INI study results and sending notices with 30-60 day correction periods, the committee voted unanimously to direct staff to create enforcement language with fines not to exceed $100/day for violations.

discussions.item: Water and Sewer Connection Agreement - Radkkey Avenue
discussions.body: Michael presented an agreement for a duplex at 2230 Radkkey Avenue to temporarily connect via long lateral until Radkkey Avenue reconstruction. Committee added language requiring assessment payment when Radkkey is reconstructed no later than 2029. Motion passed unanimously.

The committee approved an intergovernmental agreement with Rothschild for Military Road construction. Weston's utility costs are $94,860 ($6,156 sanitary, $88,704 water main). Steam Construction will hold the contract through Rothschild. Water main relocation is needed due to storm sewer conflicts. Motion passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Construction Services for Alderson/Jelnik and Bladel Projects
discussions.body: Michael presented proposals for construction inspection services. For Alderson/Jelnik roundabout, MSA proposed $37,315 versus Clark Dietz at $48,910; staff recommended MSA. For Bladel Avenue, MSA proposed $70,400 with summer intern staffing, Clark Dietz proposed $73,730 with full-time staff; staff recommended Clark Dietz for the additional experience. Committee unanimously approved MSA for roundabout and Clark Dietz for Bladel.

actionItems: Award 2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel at $4.23/ton (estimated $126,900)
Recommend village board adopt Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding
Direct staff to create enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations with fines not to exceed $100/day
Approve temporary water/sewer connection agreement for 2230 Radkkey Avenue with assessment due upon reconstruction no later than 2029
Approve intergovernmental agreement with Rothschild for Military Road construction ($94,860 village share)
Approve MSA for Alderson/Jelnik roundabout construction services ($37,315)
Approve Clark Dietz for Bladel Avenue construction services ($73,730)
Schedule Bladel Avenue special assessment meeting for May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM
Staff to provide cost analysis of clear water infiltration treatment costs at next meeting
Staff to investigate adding street lighting to Scoffield Avenue (Birch to Camp Phillips) as part of 2030 project

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its reorganization meeting, electing new officers and approving a $5,000 grant for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission also heard that a competing balloon event in Marshfield was canceled, potentially increasing attendance at the Weston event.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
New business - Grant request: Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Remarks from staff and commission members

discussions.body: The commission elected officers for the reorganization period. Trustee Erling was nominated for Chair and approved unanimously. Cushing was nominated for Vice Chair, seconded by Jackson, and approved unanimously. Renee was nominated and approved unanimously as Secretary.
The minutes from the April 21st meeting were approved. Motion was seconded by Renee and passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Grant Request - Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill (3308 Green Pastures Lane) represented the Taste and Glow Balloonfest, which had approximately 11,000-12,000 attendees last year. He announced that the competing Marshfield balloon event was canceled due to permit issues, making theirs the sole hot air balloon event in the area. A commissioner noted they requested 20 fewer rooms than last year despite receiving $5,000 previously. The commission requested better tracking of hotel room usage for future applications. Motion to approve $5,000 grant passed unanimously, matching last year's amount.

Renee reported on attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar. She described meeting the candidates, noting the winner Anastasia attended South Dakota State and had three bachelor's degrees. The candidates toured Marathon County locations including Mullen's Cheese. This was the first time the event was hosted in Marathon County since the 1970s.

actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant to Taste and Glow Balloonfest for their annual event
Taste and Glow Balloonfest to provide hotel room usage data in their post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved site plans for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and adopted a new salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection districts. Both items passed unanimously.

agenda.item: Call to Order and Roll Call
Site Plan Review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court (Item 6, taken out of order)
Unfinished Business - Salt Storage Shed Inspection Program (Item 5)
April 2026 Staff Approved Certified Survey Maps and Site Plans
Planning and Development Department Monthly Project Update

discussions.item: Site Plan Review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive. Jennifer explained the building was rotated to address challenges with a 36-inch gas pipeline running through the property, allowing the parking lot to be built over the pipeline while keeping the building on the higher elevation. Police Chief Jeremy confirmed the building will accommodate 50-60 officers for future growth beyond current staffing of 40 officers. Discussion included an alternate storage shed location and potential future expansion to the north. A motion to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees with staff conditions 2-5 passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Salt Storage Shed Inspection Program - Wellhead Protection District
discussions.body: Staff proposed a program with two annual inspections per facility - a warm weather inspection focused on structural integrity and a winter operational inspection focused on material handling practices. Michael noted the village's wells are shallow (90-110 ft) in sandy soil, making them susceptible to contamination. Staff identified one existing facility near Sternberg that predates the wellhead protection ordinance and has never gone through the conditional use process. Commission agreed staff should reach out to bring that facility into compliance. A motion to approve the program with reporting back to both Plan Commission and Public Works passed unanimously.
Minutes were approved as presented following a motion and second. Passed unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Staff Approved Certified Survey Maps and Site Plans
discussions.body: April 2026 staff-approved items were acknowledged. Motion to approve passed unanimously.

Jennifer announced the first meeting of the joint Plan Commission-Village Board steering committee for the comprehensive plan is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. Commission members requested email notification and calendar invites.

publicComment: Jim Penson of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via remote connection. He asked the commission to use common sense regarding landscaping requirements for the police department project, referencing the Green Heck Turner building precedent where landscaping standards were relaxed. He also requested that the salt storage shed inspection program include annual reporting on site visits and findings.
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive recommended for Board of Trustees approval with conditions 2-5
Salt storage shed inspection program adopted for wellhead protection district with twice-annual inspections
Staff to report inspection findings to Plan Commission and Public Works committee next year
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility near Sternberg that has not gone through wellhead protection conditional use process
First comprehensive plan steering committee meeting scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Staff to send Ehlers presentation on residential TIF districts to commission members
Electric vehicle charging stations to be discussed at future meeting in fall

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an introductory meeting for new board members, featuring educational presentations from department heads covering the Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Services, and the Aging Disability Resource Center. The committee approved the April 1st meeting minutes unanimously and received comprehensive overviews of departmental missions, budgets, and current initiatives including homelessness response efforts and veteran suicide prevention programs.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call (noting remote participation)
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operational functions required by statute - no items presented
Review of standing committee ordinance section 2.04 - missions, duties, responsibilities

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavalli made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. The motion carried unanimously with no opposition.

discussions.item: Review of Standing Committee Ordinance Section 2.04
discussions.body: Administrator Lance Leonard explained the committee's jurisdiction, including oversight of the Veteran Service Department, Social Services Department, and Health Department, plus affiliated entities North Central Health Care and the Aging Disability Resource Center. He outlined the committee's relationship with subordinate bodies including the Social Services Board, Veteran Service Commission, Transportation Coordinating Committee, and Marathon County Board of Health. Chair Boots noted the committee will be working on the strategic plan, targeting completion by December.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented an overview of the department's 37.6 FTE staff organized into environmental health, nursing, community health improvement, and administrative teams. She highlighted the department's 1,200+ licenses for restaurants, pools, and campgrounds, the Nurse Family Partnership program with its first graduates, and new public health vending machines stocked with free tick kits and other supplies. Administrator Leonard noted approximately half of the department's $5 million budget comes from tax levy, with significant funding from public charges for services and intergovernmental grants.

Administrator Leonard presented on behalf of Director Christa (not present), describing the department's nearly 140 FTE staff working in child welfare, youth justice, children's long-term support, economic support, and child support. He highlighted the successful Family Keys pilot program addressing housing instability for child welfare and youth justice clients, noting the program substantively changed based on board feedback. The department's 2026 budget totals over $22 million with approximately $7 million in tax levy.

Veteran Service Officer Erin Galindo reported the department now has 3.0 FTE after receiving board approval to increase from 2.75. He noted customer interactions jumped from 5,900 in 2024 to 8,149 in 2025. The department facilitated $44 million annually in veteran benefits and $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025. Galindo discussed major initiatives including housing stability partnerships with the Center for Veterans Issues, suicide prevention efforts (noting 7 of 30 county suicides in 2025 were veterans), and potential application for the Staff Sergeant Gordon Fox federal suicide prevention grant.

Executive Director Mike Ray explained the ADRC serves four counties (Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, and Wood) with about 60 FTEs plus 385-400 volunteers. He described services including information and assistance, caregiver support, dementia care, falls prevention, and Meals on Wheels nutrition programs. Ray noted significant financial challenges with nutrition costs up 40-45% over four years while federal funding remained flat due to the Older Americans Act not being reauthorized for two years. Marathon County's contribution is $413,135. The organization hadn't requested a levy increase from 2011 until 2024.

actionItems: April 1st committee meeting minutes approved
North Central Healthcare to be scheduled for a separate full presentation before the committee
ADRC Executive Director to present annual report with 2025 data to the full board
Chair Boots to bring strategic plan discussion items to upcoming meetings
Next meeting confirmed for Tuesday, June 3rd at 3:00 PM (corrected from initially stated May 29th)

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees, passed ordinances on mobile food vendors and e-bikes, and discussed ongoing challenges with the farmers market relocation to Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026 and approved multiple license renewals.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order and roll call
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees (Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015)
Approval of meeting minutes from April 13 and April 21, 2026
Acknowledge reports from boards, committees, and commissions with chair updates
Work product transmittals including building permits and budget status
Ordinances including mobile food vendor, nuisances, e-bikes, and transportation funding resolution
Discussion on farmers market location at Kennedy Park

discussions.item: Public Hearing on Park and Recreation Impact Fees
discussions.body: Staff presented proposed fee increases from $300 to $761 for single family units, $250 to $563 for duplex units, and $250 to $495 for multifamily units, based on the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel spoke during public comment criticizing the lack of detailed cost data justifying the fees. Trustee Clark expressed support, noting fees align with other municipalities and population growth requires park investment. Ordinance 26-010 was approved with motion by Lope Sorrell, second by Zagami, passed unanimously. Resolution 2026-015 was approved with motion by Hermling, second by Clark, passed unanimously.

The board debated amendments to the remote attendance policy in the elected officials handbook. Discussion centered on removing language requiring members to justify health, safety, or welfare reasons for remote attendance. Trustee Beck and others argued the policy should match what was previously voted on - simply requiring 12-hour notice, camera on, and no closed session participation. After debate, an amendment was made to strike sentences requiring justification for remote attendance. The amendment passed with two opposed (Hermling and Motti). The original motion as amended also passed with the same two opposed.

President appointed Stephanie Daniels (currently on Finance and HR) and Golden Mua to the CLIPS committee. Motion by Hermling, second by Lopes, approved unanimously. Parks, Finance/HR, and CLIPS still have openings. Board agreed to include committee applicant resumes in future packets.

Chief Finkey explained the ordinance creates reciprocal fire inspection recognition among Safer communities, Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, and others. A food truck inspected by any participating department receives a sticker valid across all jurisdictions, reducing redundant inspections. Motion by Lopes, second by Zagami, passed unanimously.

Board approved secondhand article and jewelry dealer license for Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC at 5703 Jamar Street. Discussion clarified state statute requirements for tracking used merchandise to prevent sale of stolen goods. Motion was amended to require satisfactory background check completion and receipt of occupancy permit, as the building is still under construction. Passed unanimously as amended.

Board discussed challenges with the farmers market's relocation to Kennedy Park after losing the Camp Phillips/Scoffield Avenue location to development. Trustee Beck raised safety concerns about cars parking on grass along Alderson Street. Trustee Clark noted the previous location was donated for $1/year and they had no choice when the owner decided to develop. Discussion of Mont Miller Park as alternative was dismissed due to concerns about low visibility reducing attendance. Renee explained the market has grown from 25 to 53 vendor spots and vendors appreciate having vehicles with them. Financial concerns were addressed - the market operates at a profit through vendor fees ($20/day or $150/season) and grants cover token programs. No action taken; discussion only.

During public comment, Jim Pitel criticized the village for hiring outside engineering consultants at $108,000 while an engineering technician position remains unfilled. Public Works Director Michael Dolan explained the position has been advertised multiple times but the specific skill set is scarce in central Wisconsin, with even consulting firms losing local employees to southeastern Wisconsin.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 52nd Street offered public comment on multiple topics: criticized the $108,000 expenditure on outside engineering consultants when an engineering technician position remains unfilled, questioned the administrator's accountability, complained that Lisa Beck was introduced as a Safer board member before official appointment, criticized lack of Kennedy Park information in the packet, and during the park fees hearing questioned whether the village has adequate data on park maintenance costs to justify the fee increases.
actionItems: Park and recreation impact fees increased effective immediately per Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015
Remote meeting attendance policy amended to remove justification requirements - members need only provide 12-hour notice, be on camera, and cannot participate in closed session
Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua appointed to CLIPS committee
Mobile food vendor license ordinance adopted creating reciprocal fire inspection recognition
Secondhand dealer license approved for Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC pending background check and occupancy permit
Board of Review adjourned to June 15, 2026
Comprehensive Plan steering committee meeting scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 6:00 PM
Staff to include committee applicant resumes in future board packets
Speed limit study on Weston Avenue to be brought back to board after construction completion
Intergovernmental service agreement to be developed for building inspection assistance to Town of Ringle for tornado-damaged homes

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board of Supervisors approved committee appointments for the new term, elected Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to at-large executive committee positions, and unanimously passed a resolution to reduce landfill tipping fees for disaster debris following the severe weather emergency that devastated parts of Ringle and other communities. The fee reduction, from the standard $65/ton to $36.94/ton for storm-related waste, will remain in effect through the state's emergency declaration period ending June 17th.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Reading of notice and roll call
Committee appointments by county board chair
Election of at-large executive committee members
Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution 26-26: Modification to landfill tipping fees

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs outlined the goals guiding appointments, including increasing diversity of perspectives, ensuring leadership development opportunities for newer supervisors (34% in first or second term), and honoring supervisor preferences. Vice Chair Boots assisted in developing the appointments. Supervisor Cavelli moved, Supervisor Crims seconded. Motion carried but was not unanimous. Supervisor Mask noted the system marked him absent but he voted aye.

discussions.item: Election of at-large executive committee members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Vice Chair Boots). Supervisor Rosenberg explained her nomination of Leur, citing her advocacy for the county's youngest and oldest residents. Paper ballots were distributed and counted. Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the executive committee.
Supervisor Cavelli moved, Supervisor Drebeck seconded. Motion carried unanimously with a voice vote.

discussions.item: Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase
discussions.body: Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. Motion carried but was not unanimous.
Motion by Supervisor P, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. Motion carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Resolution 26-26: Modification to landfill tipping fees
discussions.body: Administrator Leonard and Director Hagen Busher presented the proposal from the Solid Waste Management Board to reduce tipping fees for disaster debris from the standard $65/ton to $36.94/ton (after state waiver of $13.10 and Ringle's waiver of $2.33). Leonard noted the county will not meet the $670,743 damage threshold for state disaster aid but expects Ringle to qualify. The board suspended rules (unanimous) to consider the resolution since it hadn't gone through a standing committee. Public speakers Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck requested the county waive fees entirely and extend the timeframe to six months. Multiple supervisors asked questions about fee structures and coverage. Supervisor Arstead moved, Supervisor Morash seconded. Resolution passed unanimously.

publicComment: Two residents were granted special permission to speak on the landfill tipping fee resolution. Jessica Toasty requested the county waive all fees entirely for six months, noting that the state, DNR, and Ringle are waiving their fees, and that businesses have donated approximately $40,000 in cleanup efforts. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of affected homeowners, noting about 44 homes damaged and many still waiting on insurance adjusters, requesting fee relief for underinsured residents.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved for the new term with Vice Chairs to shepherd strategic plan updates
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to at-large executive committee positions
Reduced landfill tipping fee of $36.94/ton for disaster debris through June 17th state emergency declaration period
Administrator Leonard to continue discussions with Community Foundation about mechanism for reimbursing landowners' unreimbursed insurance costs
Solid Waste Management Board to develop long-term policy on handling disaster-related tipping fees within six months
Staff to track all storm-related waste with certification forms for potential FEMA documentation

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions unanimously and voted to forward a modified fee waiver resolution for tornado-affected properties to the county board. The committee also received educational presentations from the Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning departments.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing: Tim Veland rezoning petition (urban residential/general agriculture to rural residential) - Town of Green Valley
Public hearing: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture) - Town of McMillan
Consideration of fee waivers for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Educational presentation: Solid Waste Department overview
Educational presentation: Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department overview
Educational presentation: Conservation Planning and Zoning Department overview

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition - Town of Green Valley
discussions.body: Land Use Specialist Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from urban residential and general agriculture to rural residential for a proposed land division. The Town of Green Valley recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public testimony was offered for, against, or as interested. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact, conclusions of law, and town approval, seconded by Supervisor Kurt. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition - Town of McMillan
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the petition to rezone from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy nature, and Pogle explained the property contains a home and horse riding stable that are non-conforming uses under conservancy zoning - rezoning would make them conforming. He confirmed no natural areas would be lost. The Town of McMillan recommended approval on April 20, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Sefelt moved to approve and forward to county board, seconded by Supervisor Drew. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waivers for April 17, 2026 severe weather impacted properties
discussions.body: Director Lori Mskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and septic system fees for properties damaged by the April 17 tornado affecting towns of Ringle, Easton, and villages of Kronenwetter and Weston. County Administrator Lance clarified this applies only to county-controlled fees, not state fees or permit requirements. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future events. Supervisor Drebeck expressed concern about fairness to individual tornado victims not in declared disaster areas. After debate, Supervisor Drebeck proposed an amendment to limit the waiver to only reconnect fees ($160) for septic systems, excluding all other fees. The amendment was seconded by Supervisor Kurt and passed. The amended resolution was then approved to forward to the county board, though not unanimously.

Director David Hagenbusher presented an overview of the department's operations, including the landfill facility active since 1980, the renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy generating royalties for the county, and upcoming leachate treatment requirements by 2032 due to PFAS concerns. He noted significant capital expenses including $6 million for 11-acre expansion and equipment costs of $1.5 million per compactor. Marilyn Bend commended the department for keeping independent landfills competitive and helping Ringle tornado victims.

discussions.item: Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department presentation
discussions.body: County Forest Administrator Tom Lovelin presented on behalf of Director Jamie, covering the joint city-county department with 44 full-time and 120 seasonal employees managing 17 county parks, 30,700 acres of county forest, and city of Wausau facilities. He noted Nine Mile County Forest generates $3.25 million annually from out-of-town visitors. Supervisor Sefelt announced Tom Lovelin's retirement effective June 1st after nearly 30 years of service. Supervisor Drebeck confirmed horseback riding remains permitted at Big Eau Pleine park.

actionItems: Forward Tim Veland rezoning petition (urban residential/general agriculture to rural residential in Town of Green Valley) to Marathon County Board of Supervisors for approval
Forward Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture in Town of McMillan) to Marathon County Board of Supervisors for approval
Forward amended resolution to county board waiving only reconnect fees ($160) for septic systems on properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Town of Green Valley and Town of McMillan recommended to update comprehensive plans to reflect zoning changes if applicable
Joe Tucker selected as new County Forest Administrator following Tom Lovelin's June 1st retirement

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks Committee deferred action on the dog day at the aquatic center proposal, approved installation of kayak launch signs for the Eau Claire River, and received updates on Kennedy Park fundraising and the Yellow Banks disc golf course tree removal. The committee also discussed graffiti issues on the Oak Clair River Trail and potential food allergy awareness signage for playgrounds.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog Day at the aquatic center discussion
Graffiti on the Oak Clair River Trail discussion

discussions.body: A motion was made and seconded to approve the minutes. The motion carried with no opposition or discussion.

Director Sean Oster Bring presented a timeline document showing the project history from the comprehensive outdoor recreation plan approval in November 2023 through the May 9th ribbon cutting. Committee member Scott asked clarifying questions about funding timelines, noting he understood the project could take 10-20 years. Oster Bring explained the village had approximately $7 million responsibility for infrastructure, with Friends of Kennedy Park handling fundraising. The Friends group currently has $84,000-$85,000 at the community foundation toward a $117,000 goal for field lights, and plans to make a public check presentation at their first June tournament. The committee chair emphasized that Friends of Kennedy Park deserves grace as volunteers and shouldn't be accused of withholding information.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Dan presented photos and a PDF showing the location of fallen trees removed from the river and the installation of wood mats to protect gas pipelines during equipment access. The mats were described as approximately 8x8 posts with lifting rings. TC Energy (formerly ANR Pipeline/ATC) representatives were on-site during the work and conducted a walkthrough afterward. The village now owns these mats with extras available for future trail projects.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: The committee deferred this item to next month because Committee Chair Katrina was absent due to an unforeseen issue. A resident named Andy from 6504 [street inaudible] had spoken during public comment expressing strong opposition, stating 'There should not be animals allowed into a public pool. Period.' The chair noted Andy's objection would be recorded and he was invited to return next month for the discussion.

discussions.item: Graffiti on the Oak Clair River Trail
discussions.body: Staff explained challenges removing graffiti from concrete and memorial benches due to limited trail access. The pressure washer on a tandem axle trailer cannot reach the trail, and bridges only support pickup trucks. Committee members suggested anti-vandalism coatings, luster seal, or volunteer workdays for maintenance. Staff will investigate options and potentially address items one at a time during off-season. No formal action was taken.

Dan from 2625 Northwestern Avenue presented version 7 of proposed signage for Eau Claire River kayak launches. Three signs will be installed in Village of Weston at Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks locations. Signs include a river gauge QR code and Marathon County Parks Foundation donation URL. Dan noted the Highway J launch may be closed all season due to bridge replacement. Motion to approve installation of the signs passed unanimously.

Staff requested discussion on food allergy awareness signage for playgrounds after receiving an email from an individual offering to provide signs. The committee agreed to add this to a future agenda for discussion along with the deferred dog day item.

publicComment: Two public comments were offered. First, a resident named Andy from 6504 [street inaudible] spoke in-person opposing dogs at the aquatic center, stating animals should not be in public pools and belong in rivers and lakes. Second, Jim Pensan from 5002 Street Weston spoke virtually (using the name 'Vern M' due to login requirements) criticizing the 4 p.m. meeting time and questioning Kennedy Park financials, specifically the $8,700 expenditure figure and asking about the location of grant money and Glow event proceeds. He called the numbers 'convoluted and misleading.'
actionItems: Dog Day at the aquatic center discussion deferred to June meeting when Chair Katrina can be present
Yellow Banks kayak launch signs approved for installation at Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks locations
Staff to investigate anti-vandalism coatings and cleaning options for Oak Clair River Trail benches and structures
Food allergy awareness playground signage to be added to future meeting agenda
Mock Miller master plan kickoff meeting with Rettler scheduled for next Tuesday
Next meeting scheduled for June 22, 2026
Bike and pedestrian plan open house scheduled for June 11 from 5-7 p.m. in training room

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee approved previous meeting minutes and acknowledged financial reports including budget status and TIF district reports. The meeting featured an extensive educational presentation from Village Clerk Pam on the municipal clerk's office operations, covering elections, licensing, tax collection, and staffing challenges. Committee member Evan announced his resignation from the committee.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
TIF one and TIF two detail reports for April
Educational presentation - Municipal Clerk's Office budget and operations
Staff remarks and farmers market discussion

discussions.item: Approval of minutes from previous meeting
discussions.body: Motion to approve minutes was made by Ernling and seconded by Daniels. The motion passed unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: TIF one and TIF two detail reports
discussions.body: A committee member asked about the final numbers for TIF closure. Jessica explained they are anticipating October/November for bond payoff, noting the CDA will need to pass a resolution to call the bonds. She confirmed the village will be able to pay off the debt and described the process involving Ellers and DTC for notifying bond holders.

discussions.item: Acknowledgment of work product transmittals (items 5, 6, and 7)
discussions.body: Motion to acknowledge the April budget status report, TIF detail reports, and legal details report was made by Daniels and seconded by Soaper. Motion carried unanimously.

Village Clerk Pam presented an overview of the clerk's office operations with three staff members (two full-time, one part-time). She detailed election administration including Badger Book electronic poll books ($11,000 for four units), explaining postage costs jumped from $6,000 to $15,000 due to fall elections with over 700 absentee applications already on file. She noted poll worker wages are average at $12/hour but chief inspector pay at $13/hour is below average and will likely be increased to $15/hour next term. Pet licensing was brought back in-house from Pet Data, saving approximately $5,495 annually. The clerk's office handles 84% of tax collection ($22 million) at the counter. Newsletter costs total approximately $30,000 annually for printing and postage to 5,089 postal patrons.

Staff member Renee reported the farmers market has been going well with full vendor capacity. She noted some Tuesday vendors had to leave due to wind damaging their plants and tents. The market has expanded to include coffee vendors, new food trucks, and craft vendors. Produce vendors will increase mid to late June. A committee member raised concerns about parking on Alderson Street, and staff clarified parking is only allowed on the west side while the east side remains no parking.
Evan announced he is stepping down from the committee as he and his wife are moving away from Weston, though staying in the metro area. Committee members thanked him for his service.

actionItems: CDA resolution needed this summer to call TIF bonds due October 1st
Chief inspector wages to be reviewed for increase to $15/hour at next election term
Committee seeking citizen volunteers to fill open spots on Finance and HR Committee and other committees
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30 PM

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee addressed several regulatory matters, recommending approval of nuisance ordinance amendments, e-bike regulations with emoto restrictions, mobile food vendor fire inspections, address corrections, and a raze order for a long-abandoned property. Public comment raised concerns about the new farmers market location potentially damaging grass and ADA compliance issues with parking.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Acknowledgment of department reports (Code Enforcement, Fire/EMS, Police)
Discussion on proposed amendments to Chapter 50 nuisances
Discussion on proposed e-bicycle ordinance in Chapter 82
Discussion on overnight parking regulations October 31st to May 1st
Discussion on crosswalk request on Alderson Street
Discussion on mobile food vendors fire inspection amendment
Discussion on address corrections for E911 compliance
Discussion on raze request for 4104 Shore Avenue

discussions.body: Roman (staff) reported that code enforcement cases with suspended enforcement may move back to pending status now that construction season has begun. He announced that 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the code enforcement roll after the property sale was completed and the new owner has submitted permits for repairs. Chief Hunt noted the police department had 2,394 calls in March (77 per day), 410 traffic stops, and 281 citations issued. The department has hired its 40th officer, with the 38th starting last week, 39th starting in May, and 40th around January. Two SROs work on zoning violations during summer for all three municipalities. Motion to acknowledge reports passed unanimously.

The committee reviewed final amendments to Chapter 50 nuisances, including a previous revision switching tree limb heights to 14 and 10 feet over streets and walkways, with added language requiring compliance with the jurisdiction having authority. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees passed unanimously.

Extensive discussion occurred regarding emotos (electric dirt bike-style vehicles). Chief Hunt strongly advocated prohibiting emotos on roadways and sidewalks, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes that pose safety hazards. Committee member Sherwanka agreed emotos are dangerous. Discussion also addressed whether electric scooters used by middle schoolers should be restricted, with Chief Hunt suggesting exempting 'kids play scooters' and using officer discretion. The committee agreed to add language prohibiting emotos from operating on roadways, sidewalks, trails, or any public property. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Sherwanka, to recommend approval with the emoto prohibition passed unanimously.

Member Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour no-parking window (4am-4pm) is far outside the norm of surrounding municipalities, which typically have 4-5 hour windows overnight. Michael (DPW) noted the current even/odd system was adopted because plowing rarely occurs before 4-5am, but acknowledged only 6-8 problem areas exist. Chief Hunt opposed eliminating overnight parking entirely, preferring uniform enforcement that gives officers time to clear cars before morning plowing. After extensive debate about uniformity, exceptions for multi-family housing, and enforcement challenges, the committee decided to table the item until July so Michael can identify specific problem areas requiring signage.

Michael presented a request from Mount Olive Church for a crosswalk between the church and DC Everest school parking lot. Committee members expressed reluctance to pay for crosswalks upon request, noting no accidents have occurred and the church could cost-share. Chief Hunt had no safety concerns. The committee agreed that if Mount Olive is willing to cover the installation cost (approximately $2,000-3,000), staff can proceed. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Sherwanka, to recommend board approval contingent on Mount Olive covering costs passed unanimously.

Chief Hunt explained a new regional fire inspection program where Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, and Weston fire departments will share inspections - one inspection earns a sticker valid for all participating municipalities. Fire Marshal Marty already conducted one inspection that failed due to improper galvanized steel LP piping and unsecured propane containers. Chief Hunt noted Wausau opted out to do their own inspections. The inspection is free for vendors. Motion by Sherwanka, seconded by Campkey, to approve ordinance 26-8 passed unanimously.

Staff presented three addressing errors: 5915 Delicoski Street (duplex needing separate addresses, owner agreed), Ross Avenue properties out of sequence with an undisclosed apartment unit, and 2211 Radkey Avenue needing even-number correction for a new duplex. Chief Hunt added that Weston Villa Apartments on Delicoski has eight apartments with eight separate addresses plus a utility room with its own address, which is problematic. Motion to recommend approval to the Board passed unanimously.

Staff requested authorization to begin raze proceedings on an abandoned structure. The property has been abandoned since 2010-2011, posted for demolition since 2021, and was previously considered for a fire training burn but deemed too unsafe. Staff discovered chickens were being housed inside during winter. The current owners prefer the village proceed with demolition, with costs placed on the tax roll. Owners are current on taxes. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke regarding several agenda items. He criticized the new farmers market location at Kennedy Park, predicting the grassy areas will be destroyed and noting that parking vehicles on grass appears to violate village code. He questioned whether ADA-compliant handicap spaces should be on asphalt rather than grass for wheelchair accessibility. He questioned where the new mobile food vendor fire inspection requirement originated. He raised concerns about food truck permitting on public property versus the previous private property arrangement. He also criticized the age of some code enforcement cases, stating some have been pending for years with no visible progress on at least three cases he's aware of.
actionItems: Recommend Chapter 50 nuisance amendments to Board of Trustees for approval
Recommend e-bicycle ordinance with emoto prohibition on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property to Board of Trustees
Michael (DPW) to identify 6-8 specific problem areas for winter parking signage and bring back to July meeting
Michael to contact Mount Olive Church regarding cost-sharing for crosswalk installation on Alderson Street
Approve ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspection program
Recommend address corrections for E911 compliance to Board of Trustees
Authorize staff to begin raze order proceedings for 4104 Shore Avenue
Staff to investigate property at Sternberg and South Timber with furniture and dumpster outside
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, July 7th at 5:00 PM

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on Employee Handbook Updates for 2026-27
Board was expected to vote on the Preliminary 2026-27 Budget recommendation
Board was expected to vote on 2026-2027 Paid Meal Price Update
Board was expected to vote on Activities & Athletics Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling revisions
Board was expected to vote on Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Riverview RFP Approval for HVAC and roofing project

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan
Board was expected to vote on 2026-27 Teacher Contract Approvals
Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff
Board was expected to vote on WAMCS Charter School Contract Renewal for 2026-2031
Board was expected to potentially take action following closed session regarding non-renewal notices and contract evaluations

The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.

Special assessments levied for 2025 Street Construction Projects
